mnemonics.title: Types of Renewable Energy
mnemonics.content: SWB-GTO: Solar, Wind, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Ocean (thermal)

mnemonics.title: Biogas Composition
mnemonics.content: MUST: Methane (60-70%), Unexplained gases (traces), Sulfur (small), Trace CO2 (30-40%)

mnemonics.title: Renewable Energy Advantages
mnemonics.content: SENSE: Sustainable, Environmentally clean, Non-depleting, Secure, Economic

acronyms.usage: Refers to solar cells that convert sunlight directly into electricity
Technology that generates electricity from ocean temperature differences
Not a renewable but often compared with biogas as alternative fuel
Natural gas stored at high pressure, compared with biogas
System using solar energy to heat water for domestic/industrial use

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a sandwich: top glass layer (protection) → semiconductor silicon (middle, absorbs light) → metal backing (bottom, conducts electricity). Photons from sun knock electrons loose, creating DC current.
Think of a giant electric fan in reverse. Instead of using electricity to make wind, wind makes electricity. Blades catch wind → spin shaft → generator produces electricity.
A digestive system for waste. Input chamber (feeding) → digester (stomach where bacteria work without oxygen) → gas holder (breath out biogas) → outlet (slurry as fertilizer.
Like a battery that never dies while fed hydrogen. Hydrogen enters one side, oxygen enters other → they meet at electrolyte → electricity flows out → water comes out as exhaust.

analogies.analogy: Like a rechargeable battery vs a disposable battery. Renewable energy sources recharge themselves naturally (like solar recharges each day), while fossil fuels are like a one-time-use battery that depletes.
Like charging a phone during the day but using it at night. Solar energy must be stored in batteries for use when the sun isn't shining.
Biomass is like a concentrated food bar - compact and storable, while solar and wind are like eating fresh fruits - abundant but spread out and hard to store.
Like using the residual heat from a still-warm oven long after turning it off. The Earth retains heat deep inside that can be tapped for energy.
